title: The Woodlark
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Teevo cheetio cheevio chee:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ O where, what can thן¿½t be?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weedio-weedio: there again!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So tiny a trickle of sן¿½ng-strain;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all round not to be found
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For brier, bough, furrow, or grן¿½en ground
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Before or behind or far or at hand
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Either left either right
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Anywhere in the sן¿½nlight.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, after all! Ah but hark - 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ 'I am the little woodlark.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To-day the sky is two and two
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With white strokes and strains of the blue
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Round a ring, around a ring
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And while I sail (must listen) I sing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The skylark is my cousin and he
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is known to men more than me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  . . . when the cry within
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Says Go on then I go on
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till the longing is less and the good gone

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But down drop, if it says Stop,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the all-a-leaf of the trן¿½etop
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And after that off the bough
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I ן¿½m so vן¿½ry, O sן¿½ very glad
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That I dן¿½ thן¿½nk there is not to be had . . .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The blue wheat-acre is underneath
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the braided ear breaks out of the sheath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ear in milk, lush the sash,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And crush-silk poppies aflash,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The blood-gush blade-gash
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flame-rash rudred
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bud shelling or broad-shed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tatter-tassel-tangled and dingle-a-dangled
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dandy-hung dainty head.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And down ... the furrow dry
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sunspurge and oxeye
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And laced-leaved lovely
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Foam-tuft fumitory
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through the velvety wind V-winged
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the nest's nook I balance and buoy
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a sweet joy of a sweet joy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sweet, of a sweet, of a sweet joy
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of a sweet - a sweet - sweet - joy.'
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Gerard Manley Hopkins delivers a powerful performance in "The Woodlark"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
